problem: Find the absolute value of the difference of single-digit integers $A$ and $B$ such that  $$ \begin{array}{c@{}c@{\;}c@{}c@{}c@{}c} & & & B& B & A_6\\ & & & \mathbf{4} & \mathbf{1} & B_6\\& & + & A & \mathbf{1} & \mathbf{5_6}\\ \cline{2-6} & & A & \mathbf{1} & \mathbf{5} & \mathbf{2_6} \\ \end{array} $$Express your answer in base $6$.

classified_questions.question: What is the first step taken when solving the problem?
classified_questions.label: f

classified_questions.question: In which column did you begin your calculations? Is there a specific reason you chose to start there?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: If $A_6+B_6+5_6$ had equaled $22_6$ instead of $12_6$, what would that have meant for the values of $A_6$ and $B_6$?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Why is it impossible for $A_6+B_6$ to equal $13_6$? Can this be generalized to other base systems?
classified_questions.label: e

classified_questions.question: What is the significance of the fact that $B_6$ equals $2_6$ in the solution? Could there have been other values that worked?
classified_questions.label: e

classified_questions.question: Are there any other methods you could have used to solve the problem? How do you know that this method is the most efficient?
classified_questions.label: e
